skill.name: secrets-management
skill.description: Implement secrets management patterns for CI/CD pipelines using Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GitHub Secrets, GitLab variables, and Kubernetes External Secrets.
skill.summary: Guide secure CI/CD secret storage, retrieval, rotation, and scanning workflows.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly found many command, network, and credential patterns, but they appear inside SKILL.md documentation examples rather than executable skill code. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found, but several examples encourage printing secrets into CI logs and should carry a marketplace warning or be fixed.

medium_findings.title: Secret Values Printed in CI Logs
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes CI examples that echo API keys, database URLs, and production API keys to job logs. This is dangerous because users may copy the examples and expose secrets through CI output even when platform secret stores are used.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly print variables named API key, database URL, and production API key in CI run steps. The context confirms this is documentation, not malicious code, but the unsafe recommendation is clear.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Demo Secrets in Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation uses a Vault root token, a literal password value, and a literal AWS secret string in examples. These are presented as setup snippets, but copying them into real environments would create weak or shared credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples contain concrete secret-like values and a root token. The Vault block is labeled as a dev server, which lowers maliciousness but does not remove the copy-paste risk.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The shell, Vault, AWS CLI, Docker, Terraform, and CI command patterns are inside Markdown code fences. No evidence found that the skill contains executable scripts or automatically runs these commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context shows fenced documentation blocks rather than runnable repository files. The commands are still relevant risk indicators because users may execute them manually.

low_findings.title: Network Indicators Use Placeholder or Local Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs and IP address point to localhost or example Vault endpoints in setup documentation. No evidence found of a hidden external collection endpoint or data exfiltration workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are standard Vault examples and include localhost or example.com hostnames. They do not appear to be attacker-controlled endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Appears False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts correspond to text in the skill description and related skill name, not to cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, DES, RC4, or similar algorithms being recommended or executed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are metadata text and a related-skill bullet. They do not contain crypto implementation guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: CI Examples Echo Secret Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: The most concerning pattern is documentation that logs secret variables in CI jobs. Marketplace users could copy these snippets and disclose credentials in build logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippets directly echo variables identified as secrets. This is a concrete unsafe pattern even though it is not malicious execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copy-Paste Shell Commands Handle Live Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples retrieve, export, or write secret values through shell commands. These examples require careful masking and redaction before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are inside documentation, but they operate on secret values and CI environments. Legitimate use is possible with stronger safeguards.

content.value_statement: CI/CD pipelines often expose credentials through hardcoded values, logs, or weak access controls. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design safer secret storage, retrieval, rotation, and scanning workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, Google Secret Manager, and platform-native secret options.
Provides CI/CD examples for GitHub Actions and GitLab pipelines.
Shows patterns for secret retrieval, masking, and environment injection.
Outlines automated and manual secret rotation workflows.
Covers Kubernetes External Secrets Operator configuration patterns.
Recommends secret scanning with tools such as TruffleHog.
limitations: It is a documentation-only skill and does not configure secret stores automatically.
Some included examples need redaction before production use because they print secret variables.
It does not verify cloud IAM policies or Vault policies against a live environment.
Provider examples are broad and may need updates for organization-specific compliance rules.

use_cases.title: Harden a CI/CD Pipeline
use_cases.description: Replace hardcoded credentials with managed secrets and safer retrieval steps.

use_cases.title: Plan Secret Rotation
use_cases.description: Create a rotation process for database passwords, API keys, or deployment credentials.

use_cases.title: Standardize Secret Scanning
use_cases.description: Add pre-commit and CI checks that block accidental secret commits.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Secret Risks
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an existing pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my CI/CD pipeline for hardcoded secrets, unsafe logging, and missing secret masking. Explain the top risks first.

prompt_templates.title: Design Secret Retrieval
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate migration to a managed store
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitHub Actions workflow that retrieves deployment secrets from Vault or AWS Secrets Manager without printing secret values.

prompt_templates.title: Build Rotation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production secret rotation plan for database credentials, including validation, rollback, audit logging, and old secret revocation.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Multi-Cloud Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, and Google Secret Manager for this CI/CD architecture. Recommend least-privilege access controls.

output_examples.input: Review a pipeline that uses repository secrets and prints environment variables during deployment.
output_examples.output: The review flags secret logging as the highest risk, recommends masking, removes direct echo commands, and suggests scoped environment secrets.

output_examples.input: Create a rotation process for a production database password.
output_examples.output: The plan covers generating a new password, updating the secret store, validating application access, monitoring errors, and revoking the old password.

output_examples.input: Add secret scanning to developer and CI workflows.
output_examples.output: The result recommends local pre-commit scanning, required CI scanning, clear failure messages, and a process for approved false positives.

best_practices: Mask and redact secret values before writing any CI output or diagnostic logs.
Use least-privilege identities and separate secrets for development, staging, and production.
Rotate secrets regularly and keep audit logs for secret access and changes.
anti_patterns: Do not echo API keys, database URLs, or tokens in pipeline logs.
Do not store production secrets in repository files or reusable sample snippets.
Do not grant CI jobs broad cloud permissions when a scoped role is sufficient.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands or connect to secret stores?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples only. Users must run and adapt commands in their own environments.

faq.question: Which secret platforms does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, Google Secret Manager, GitHub Secrets, GitLab variables, and External Secrets Operator.

faq.question: Can it help remove hardcoded secrets from CI/CD?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide migration from hardcoded values to managed secret stores and platform-native secret variables.

faq.question: Is the published skill risk-free?
faq.answer: No. The audit found unsafe logging examples that should be treated as warnings and revised before production copying.

faq.question: Does it replace a security review?
faq.answer: No. Teams should still review IAM policies, Vault policies, audit logging, and compliance requirements.

faq.question: How should users handle the example snippets?
faq.answer: Users should adapt them carefully, remove any secret-printing commands, and test with non-production credentials first.
